Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Alif, Lam, Meem. The revelation of the Book, about which there is no doubt, is from the Lord of the worlds. (1-2)

'Alif, Lam, Meem' has already been explained in Surah al-Baqarah. It should be noted that, like the preceding Surah, the introduction of this Surah closely resembles the opening of Surah al-Baqarah.

As we have clarified elsewhere, 'Tanzil' (revelation) does not simply mean sending down, but sending down gradually with special care. 'Al-Kitab' (the Book) refers to the Holy Qur'an. This means that the sending down of this Book is from the Lord of the worlds, and there is no room for any doubt about its divine origin. This is the exact meaning of 'la rayba feeh' that we explained in the commentary of Surah al-Baqarah, and this verse supports that interpretation. Both the Quraysh and the Jews disagreed most strongly with the Holy Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) claim that this Book was revealed to him by Allah. They labeled this claim as a fabrication ('iftira'), accusing the Prophet of composing this Book himself but falsely attributing it to Allah to establish dominance over them.

Or do they say, "He invented it"? Rather, it is the truth from your Lord, [O Muhammad], that you may warn a people to whom no warner has come before you so perhaps they will be guided. (3)

This question expresses extreme surprise and bewilderment: have these people become so blind and deaf in their opposition to the truth that they label the divine origin of this Book as a fabrication? It means that if there were any spark of justice and truthfulness in them, they would never have uttered such a thing; but they have become completely blind and deaf in their hostility.

'Rather, it is the truth from your Lord' (bal huwal-haqqu mir-Rabbik). The Qur'an has responded to this accusation from various angles, which have been explained in previous Surahs. Here, instead of giving a detailed reply, the claim is reinforced with a highly stern warning. This emphasis is prominent in two aspects. First, 'it is the truth' (huwal-haqq) implies that the ancestral religion they champion is completely false, and the only true religion is this one whose divine origin they deny. It is indeed from God, and if they persist in rejecting it, they will face the consequences.

'That you may warn a people to whom no warner has come before you so perhaps they will be guided.' This defines the purpose of sending down this Book with such special care: Allah has revealed it so that you may warn these people about the ultimate end of this life and the affairs of the hereafter, as no warner had come to them before you. 'A people' (qawm) refers to the unlettered Arabs, among whom no prophet had been raised since Ishmael (peace be upon him). This highlights the aspect of favor inherent in this Book: Allah has bestowed a great blessing upon the unlettered Arabs by revealing this Book to them, and they should value this magnificent favor.

At the same time, it carries a warning: when Allah sends a warner to a nation, their fate enters the balance. If they fail to reform after this, Allah inevitably destroys them after granting them a specified respite. This divine law has been explained throughout this book.

Allah is the One who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days; then He established Himself above the Throne. You have not besides Him any protector or any intercessor; so will you not be reminded? (4)

In the previous verse, the purpose of sending down this Book and the specific mission of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) were explained. Now, the details follow. The focus of the warning is on two things: monotheism and the Resurrection. First, the Qur'an warns people not to spend their lives relying on false supports and the intercession of imaginary deities. The sole Creator and Disposer of this universe is Allah alone, without any partner; everyone must return to Him and be held accountable before Him. Therefore, His gratitude, worship, and obedience are obligatory upon all.

Second, it warns that the Resurrection is an absolute certainty. Everyone will stand before God, and on that Day, the criminals will confess their crimes and beg for another chance to return to the world to live a life of faith and righteous deeds, but there will be no opportunity for such pleas and entreaties.

This and the subsequent verses discuss monotheism, after which the Resurrection and its details will be mentioned.

'Allah is the One who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days; then He established Himself above the Throne.' As explained in its respective place, 'six days' refers to divine days, and the subsequent verse clarifies that one day with Allah is equal to a thousand of our years. Therefore, six days refer to six distinct cosmic eras. This points to the reality that the creation of the heavens, the earth, and what is between them did not occur as an accidental event; rather, Allah created them through a highly gradual, orderly process. This gradual arrangement is proof of His wisdom and purpose, showing that this universe is not a playground but a purposeful creation.

'Then He established Himself above the Throne' (thumma-stawa 'alal-'Arsh) means that after creating this world with such order, Allah did not leave it to run on its own; rather, He established Himself above His Throne of authority, directly managing and directing all its affairs. This refutes the polytheists' belief that Allah, after creating the world, handed over its management to other close deities and became completely detached. The origin of this superstitious belief has been explained previously.

'You have not besides Him any protector or any intercessor' (ma lakum min doonihi miw-waliyyin-wa la shafee') presents the logical conclusion: since the reins of all affairs are directly in His hand, everyone must stand before Him, and He alone will decide all matters. On that Day, none other can protect them or intercede against His will. The word 'doon' carries the meaning of both 'besides' and 'against,' which is why the negation of both a protector and an intercessor is mentioned.

'So will you not be reminded?' (a-fa-la tatadhakkaroon). Since all these conclusions are based on the admissions of the addressees themselves, they are warned: 'Why do you not use your common sense to understand such obvious things!'

He directs the plan from the heaven to the earth; then it ascends to Him in a Day whose extent is a thousand years of those which you count. That is the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Exalted in Might, the Merciful. (5-6)

Meaning, He alone directs all affairs from the heaven to the earth. This refutes the belief of those polytheists who assumed that since the earth is a distant region of His universe, He has restricted His kingdom to the heavens and left the management of the earth to other agents. Addressing this group, the Qur'an asks elsewhere: 'Is there a god in the earth and a god in the heaven? How foolishly you judge!'

'Then it ascends to Him' (thumma ya'ruju ilayh) means all plans originate from Him and ultimately return to Him. 'Ya'ruju' carries the meaning of being presented. The meaning is that He does not simply issue decrees and remain detached; rather, everything is presented before Him, and He remains fully aware of how His decrees are executed.

'In a Day whose extent is a thousand years of those which you count' (fee yawmin kana miqdaruhu alfa sanatim-mimma ta'uddoon). Most commentators have interpreted this as the Day of Judgment, referring to the presentation of human deeds before God on that Day. Since the Day of Judgment will be extremely severe, its intensity is described metaphorically as being equal to a thousand years. In our view, this is incorrect. This exact theme appears in Surah al-Hajj as follows:

'And indeed, a day with your Lord is like a thousand years of those which you count.' (al-Hajj: 47)

In that Surah, the verse was revealed in response to the deniers' demand to hasten the punishment. When they were warned, they would say: 'We have been hearing this threat for a long time, but it has not come; if it is real, why does it not strike us?' In response, they were told: 'Do not measure God's affairs by your limited standards. Your days are twenty-four hours, which is why a few years seem like a long period to you. But one day with your Lord is like a thousand of your years, and His plans are designed according to His own cosmic days. You look at your own days and panic that a decree has not been executed, whereas in divine days, not even a single moment has passed.'

In this exact context, the verse under discussion is revealed. It means that all plans and management are in His hands. Decrees originate from Him and return to Him, but this process occurs according to divine days. Therefore, not everyone can immediately see the consequences or understand the wisdom behind His plans. The correct approach for His servants is to remain patient instead of showing haste.

'That is the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Exalted in Might, the Merciful.' He alone is the Knower of all secrets, dominant over all things, and All-Merciful. None other has the capacity to know all the mysteries of the universe. Since He is All-Knowing, Mighty, and Merciful, His servants must place their complete trust in Him.

Who perfected everything which He created and began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his posterity from an extract of a humble fluid. Then He proportioned him and breathed into him from His spirit and made for you hearing and vision and hearts; little are you grateful. (7-9)

Using His attributes of being the Knower of the unseen and witnessed, Mighty, and Merciful as a foundation, the text introduces the Resurrection. It states that He is the One who perfected everything He created—meaning every single object He created testifies to His power, wisdom, and boundless knowledge. If a human being reflects on even the smallest creation, his intellect is awed by His craftsmanship, and he instinctively exclaims: 'So blessed is Allah, the best of creators!' This opens the door to the realization that the One who possesses such power, wisdom, and mercy could never create this vast universe without a purpose. Therefore, He must establish a Day to distinguish between truth and falsehood—rewarding those who appreciated His blessings and punishing those who lived like the blind and deaf, refusing to reflect on His wisdom or listen to those who did.

'And began the creation of man from clay.' To support 'He perfected everything which He created,' the text points to human creation itself rather than external signs, implying: 'To understand this reality, you do not need to look far; reflect on the stages of your own creation and see His power, wisdom, and care!' It states that this human being, who is so proud of his capabilities today, was not originated from any valuable material, but from humble clay, from which his physical form was shaped and blessed with life. Look at the craftsmanship of the Creator: how He transformed a lump of clay into such a magnificent being!

'Then He made his posterity from an extract of a humble fluid.' This points to the second stage of human creation: his posterity was designed to continue through a humble, fluid extract rather than clay.

'Then He proportioned him and breathed into him from His spirit and made for you hearing and vision and hearts.' 'Taswiyah' (proportioning) means to refine, shape, and perfect. In art, it refers to the final 'finishing touch.' It means that in the third stage, He refined the physical form of this human and breathed into him of His spirit, endowing him with hearing, vision, and hearts—the unique faculties of perception that distinguish him from animals.

'Breathed into him from His spirit' (nafakha feehi mir-Roohih) refers to the divine spirit (the spiritual light). Alongside his animal spirit, human beings are blessed with a spark of divine light, which illuminates his hearing, vision, and heart, elevating him above other creatures. If he is deprived of this light, his inner self becomes as dark as that of animals. Animals also possess ears, eyes, and hearts, but they lack this divine light; therefore, their faculties do not possess the capabilities granted to humans. If a human being neglects this light, he sinks to the level of animals. It should be noted that preserving, increasing, or diminishing this light is within human choice: those who value it and fulfill its rights increase it, while those who neglect it eventually extinguish it.

Associating the spirit with Him ('from His spirit') denotes His special favor; it does not mean that the spirit is a physical part of Allah. This clarification is necessary to refute the pantheistic misinterpretations.

This verse shows that before proportioning and the breathing of the spirit, humans passed through a stage of physical raw development, lacking perception and wisdom. After this stage, proportioning refined his physical form, and the divine spirit illuminated his inner self.

'Little are you grateful' (qaleelam-ma tashkuroon) is a rebuke: reflect on these stages of your creation, from a humble origin to this elevated status! Your every fiber should be grateful to your Lord, utilizing the faculties He granted in His service, yet you show very little gratitude.

And they say, "When we are lost within the earth, will we indeed be in a new creation?" Rather, they are in the meeting with their Lord disbelievers. (10)

Meaning, Allah blessed them with the faculties of hearing, vision, and hearts so that they would reflect on His signs and realize that this world is not a purposeless creation, and that they must stand before Him to answer for their deeds. But instead of using these faculties, they mockingly ask: 'When we decay and turn to dust, will we indeed be resurrected?'

'Rather, they are in the meeting with their Lord disbelievers' (bal hum bi-liqa'i Rabbihim kafiroon) shows that their questions are mere excuses. Witnessing His power within themselves and in the universe, they cannot genuinely believe that He is unable to resurrect them. The actual reason is that they reject the meeting with their Lord and do not want to accept the moral responsibility that comes with it, so they fabricate these intellectual doubts.

Say, "The angel of death who has been entrusted with you will take your soul. Then to your Lord you will be returned." (11)

This is the response to both of their doubts: those who die are taken by the angel of death entrusted with them by Allah. Therefore, even after death, no one is hidden from the sight of Allah and His angels. Whenever He wills, He will resurrect them, and they will be brought before Him under the custody of His angels.

'Then to your Lord you will be returned' (thumma ila Rabbikum turja'oon) dispels their false hope of returning to their partners for intercession. On that Day, those partners will not exist; everyone will stand before Allah alone, and He will decide all matters.

'And if you could but see when the criminals hang their heads before their Lord...' (Wa law tara idhil-mujrimoona nakisoo ru'oosihim 'inda Rabbihim). The address can be to the Prophet or to the readers. In the first case, it serves as consolation, and in the second, as a warning. The response to 'if you could but see' is omitted for eloquence, implying: 'You would witness a scene of absolute helplessness that you cannot even imagine today!'

'"Our Lord, we have seen and heard, so return us [to the world]; we will do righteousness. Indeed, we are [now] certain"' (Rabbana absarna wa sami'na farji'na na'mal salihan inna mooqinoon). At that moment, they will openly confess their crimes, begging to return to the world to do good deeds because they have attained certain faith.

And if We had willed, We could have given every soul its guidance, but the word from Me will come into effect [that] "I will surely fill Hell with jinn and nations of mankind, all together." (13)

This is the response to their plea, which will be given on that Day: 'This forced confession after witnessing the reality is of no benefit. Had We willed, We could have created everyone on guidance and faith; that was not difficult for Us. But We did not do so because We granted choice to humans, to test who would use his intellect to believe and who would follow his desires to align with Satan.'

'But the word from Me will come into effect: "I will surely fill Hell with jinn and mankind, all together"' refers to the decree Allah announced to Iblees when he challenged Him. This is mentioned in various places. In Surah Sad:

'He said: "By Your Might, I will surely mislead them all, except Your chosen servants among them." He said: "Then the truth [is My oath], and the truth I say: I will surely fill Hell with you and those of them who follow you, all together."' (Sad: 82-85)

Referencing this decree is to inform the criminals that the time for excuses has passed. Allah had declared His decision that those who chose to follow Satan would be cast into Hell with him. Worldly life was the place of trial, which has now concluded, and My word has been fulfilled.

"So taste [the punishment] because you forgot the meeting of this Day of yours. Indeed, We have forgotten you. And taste the punishment of eternity for what you used to do." (14)

'Forgot' (naseetum) here carries the meaning of neglecting and ignoring. It means: 'No plea or request will be entertained now. Just as you neglected this Day in your worldly life, We have neglected you today; your pleas are unworthy of attention. Taste the eternal punishment for what you used to do.' This is no injustice, but the harvest of what they sowed throughout their lives.

2. The upcoming subject... Verses 15-22

Above, the causes of their rejection were explained. Now, the text describes those who are accepting this Book, their reward and status before Allah, and the end that awaits their opponents in this world and the hereafter. Read the verses in this light.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Only those believe in Our verses who, when they are reminded by them, fall down in prostration and exalt [Allah] with praise of their Lord, and they are not arrogant. (15)

This consoles the Prophet: 'Do not expect any good from those who are blinded by their worldly pride. Only those will believe in Our verses who possess humility and fear in their hearts. When they are reminded of their Lord and the hereafter, they do not dispute; rather, they fall down in prostration, exalting Him with praise.' This means that only those whose hearts have not been hardened will be affected by these verses; any worldly unmindfulness is immediately removed when they are reminded, and they accept the truth with ultimate devotion.

'And exalt [Allah] with praise of their Lord' (wa sabbahoo bi-hamdi Rabbihim). We have discussed the significance of combining glorification (tasbeeh) and praise (hamd). Glorification denotes negation of imperfections, and praise denotes affirmation of perfection; true recognition of Allah is attained by keeping both aspects in view. If either is ignored, a person falls into misconceptions about Him.

'And they are not arrogant' (wa hum la yastakbiroon) is the core lesson: they possess this capability because they are free from arrogance (istikbar)—which is to show pride against the truth. Arrogance prevents a person from accepting an obvious truth, and leads to being deprived of guidance. Guidance is granted only to those who bow before the truth, regardless of who presents it.

Their sides depart from [their] beds; they supplicate their Lord in fear and hope, and from what We have provided them, they spend. (16)

Since the fear of their Lord and the Resurrection is constantly on their minds, they rise at night, leaving their comfortable beds, to pray and call upon Him in fear of His wrath and hope of His mercy, and they spend in His cause from what We have provided them.

This mentions two things: prayer and charity. After faith, these two are the primary expressions of devotion, on which the entire structure of religion stands.

And no soul knows what has been hidden for them of comfort for eyes as reward for what they used to do. (17)

This is the reward for their night prayers and charity: no soul can fathom the comfort of the eyes Allah has hidden for them in the hereafter. Those who mocked them in this world for their sacrifices will be astounded, and the believers will rejoice at the boundless reward, which far exceeds their expectations.

'As reward for what they used to do' (jazā'am-bi-ma kanoo ya'maloon) means this reward is the fruit of their own actions. Though it is entirely by His grace, He describes it as the result of their actions, because a person derives the greatest joy from the results of his own efforts.

Then is he who was a believer like him who was defiantly disobedient? They are not equal. (18)

This is the proof of the reward: what has been said is absolute and just. If it were not so, it would mean that the Creator treats the believer and the wrongdoer as equal, and makes no distinction between His loyal servants and His rebels. Such an assumption contradicts His wisdom, justice, and mercy, and is obviously false. They can never be equal; therefore, a Day must be established to reward the loyal and punish the disobedient.

As for those who believed and did righteous deeds, for them are the Gardens of Refuge, as accommodation for what they used to do. (19)

This details their reward: those who lived a life of faith and righteous deeds will have the Gardens of Refuge (Jannatul-Ma'wa), which will be presented to them as their initial reception (nuzul). 'Nuzul' refers to the initial hospitality offered to a guest upon arrival. This indicates that 'Jannatul-Ma'wa' refers to those gardens where the believers will be received initially, before entering the main Paradise. Thus, these gardens serve as a 'Rest House' for them, and the plural form indicates that these gardens will be distinct for different believers. Surah an-Najm (verse 15) indicates that 'Jannatul-Ma'wa' is near 'Sidratul-Muntaha'—the boundary between the physical and spiritual realms, which is highly appropriate. These are matters of the unseen, and I have explained this to clarify the meaning of 'nuzul.' Further discussion will follow under Surah an-Najm.

But as for those who defiantly disobeyed, their refuge is the Fire. Every time they wish to get out of it, they will be returned to it and it will be said to them, "Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny." (20)

After describing the reward of the believers, this describes the punishment of the wrongdoers. Their refuge will be the Fire, where they will be cast. Their initial reception—as clear from other places—will be boiling water and the tree of Zaqqum. Whenever they try to escape from the Fire, they will be dragged back into it, and told: 'Do not try to escape; this is the very Fire you used to deny. Taste its punishment!'

And We will surely let them taste of the nearer punishment before the greater punishment that perhaps they will return. (21)

'The nearer punishment' (al-'adhabal-adna) refers to the worldly punishment sent as a warning. 'The greater punishment' (al-'adhabal-akbar) refers to the punishment of the hereafter. It states that before the greater punishment, We will surely let them taste of the nearer punishment in this world, referring to the Quraysh, who are warned that they will face punishment in this world to make them reform. This nearer punishment began with the Battle of Badr, where their chiefs were eliminated, and continued until the Conquest of Makkah when their power was shattered. Sincere among them embraced Islam, and the rebellious were eliminated. Since the majority ultimately believed, they were spared the total destruction that befell previous nations, as indicated by 'that perhaps they will return' (la'allahum yarji'oon).

And who is more unjust than one who is reminded of the verses of his Lord; then he turns away from them? Indeed We, from the criminals, will take retribution. (22)

This explains the warning of punishment: why should they not be punished! Who is more unjust than one who is reminded of His verses through His Book and Messenger, yet turns away from them? When they ignore this final warning, no other means remains to guide them; they are criminals, and We will surely take retribution from them. This has been explained in various places.

4. The upcoming subject... Verses 23-30

Next are the concluding verses of the Surah. The warning that those who reject guidance will face punishment in this world and the hereafter is substantiated through history. At the same time, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his Companions are promised ultimate victory if they stand firm. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

And We certainly gave Moses the Scripture, so do not be in doubt of its meeting. And We made it a guidance for the Children of Israel. And We made from among them leaders guiding by Our command when they were patient and were certain of Our verses. (23-24)

'Al-Kitab' refers to the Torah; those who interpret it as a general term contradict the clear Qur'anic context. 'We made it' (ja'alnahu) refers to this Book, and is defined as: 'We made it a guidance for the Children of Israel,' which proves it refers specifically to the Torah.

'Its meeting' (liqa'ihi) refers to the Day of Judgment and retribution mentioned in verses 21-22. The meaning is: just as We gave Moses the Scripture and punished its deniers in this world and the hereafter, We will surely punish those who deny the Book revealed to you. 

'So do not be in doubt of its meeting' (fa-la takun fee miryatim-min-liqa'ih) means do not doubt the occurrence of this Day of retribution. This is addressed to the Prophet, but is a warning directed at the deniers.

We have explained that the commission of a messenger and a Book requires that those who deny them face punishment. This is expressed elsewhere as:

'And We had certainly given Moses the Scripture and appointed with him his brother Aaron as a helper. And We said, "Go both of you to the people who have denied Our signs." Then We destroyed them with [complete] destruction.' (Al-Furqan: 35-36)

In Surah al-Mu'minun:

'But they denied them and were of those destroyed. And We certainly gave Moses the Scripture that perhaps they would be guided.' (al-Mu'minun: 48-49)

This is also stated as a general law for all messengers in Surah ar-Rum (verse 47). These parallels clarify the meaning of the verse, which has sometimes confused commentators who ignored the context.

'And We made it a guidance for the Children of Israel' means while the deniers faced their end, those who accepted it found it a source of guidance.

'And We made from among them leaders guiding by Our command when they were patient.' This is the fruit of the Book: Allah elevated them to leadership and raised leaders among them who guided people by His command. This shows that leadership is a natural result of adhering to His Book; as long as a nation stands by His Book, they hold leadership, and their enemies are humiliated.

'Guiding by Our command when they were patient' defines the conditions of leadership: they guided people strictly in accordance with His command, not by following desires.

'And were certain of Our verses' (wa kanoo bi-aayatina yooqinoon) is the foundation of their patience: they had certain faith in His signs and the Resurrection, which enabled them to stand firm in trials.

These verses contain glad tidings and a reminder of responsibility for the Muslims, and a rebuke to the Jews who had abandoned this high status!

Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ. (25)

Meaning, their affairs will be presented before His اخروی (ultimate) court, and He will judge between them concerning their differences, punishing them for their deviation.

Has it not guided them - how many generations We destroyed before them, [while] they walk among their dwellings? Indeed in that are signs; so will they not hear? (26)

After pointing to the opponents of Moses, the text references other past nations: 'Has it not guided them how We destroyed many generations before them, while they walk among their ruined dwellings?' This refers to 'Ad, Thamud, and Lot's people, whose ruins lay along their trade routes. The meaning is: 'Why do they not learn from history? Do they want to face the same end?'

'Indeed in that are signs' means there are great lessons in these ruins if they use their reason:

First: Those who are warned through His Book and Messenger but turn away are criminals, and He surely takes retribution from them.

Second: He punishes such criminals in this world and the hereafter.

Third: After destroying the criminals, He grants leadership to those who stand firm on the truth.

'So will they not hear?' (a-fa-la yasma'oon). 'Hear' here means to understand and learn lessons, for that is the true purpose of hearing.

Have they not seen that We drive the water to the barren land and bring forth thereby crops from which their livestock eat and themselves? So will they not see? (27)

'Barren land' (ardal-juruz) refers to dry, lifeless land. Since water-laden clouds are driven to it, 'We drive' (nasooq) is used.

After pointing to the historical signs of His law of justice, the text points to His power as proof of the Resurrection. Have they not observed how He drives clouds to dead, barren lands and revives them with rain, causing crops to grow for their sustenance and that of their livestock!

'So will they not see?' (a-fa-la yubsiroon). While the previous verse rebuked them for not hearing, this one rebukes them for not seeing. They witness this daily revival of dead land, yet they doubt His power to resurrect them.

And they say, "When is this decision, if you should be truthful?" (28)

Meaning, in their blindness, when they are referred to these signs, they mockingly ask: 'If you are indeed truthful, when is this promised Day of decision to arrive?' They refuse to believe on the basis of signs, demanding to see the physical reality.

Say, "On the Day of Decision the belief of those who disbelieved will not benefit them, nor will they be granted respite." (29)

This is the response: tell them that once the Day of Decision arrives, believing then will be of no benefit, nor will they be granted any respite to reform. This applies to both the Resurrection and the worldly punishment that strikes upon rejecting the messenger. Once the punishment arrives, forced belief holds no value in His law, which accepts only the faith chosen freely through intellect and reason.

So turn away from them and wait. Indeed, they are waiting. (30)

Finally, the Prophet is commanded: 'Since they refuse to listen and demand the Day of decision, do not engage in further debates with them. Turn away from them and wait for the Day of decision; they too are waiting. Once their specified respite is completed, that Day will surely arrive.'

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed by the grace of Allah. Praise be to Allah for this.
